Stated learning outcomes:
The goals for 18.06 are using matrices and also understanding them.
Here are key computations and some of the ideas behind them:
    Solving Ax = b for square systems by elimination (pivots, multipliers, back substitution, invertibility of A, factorization into A = LU)
    Complete solution to Ax = b (column space containing b, rank of A, nullspace of A and special solutions to Ax = 0 from row reduced R)
    Basis and dimension (bases for the four fundamental subspaces)
    Least squares solutions (closest line by understanding projections)
    Orthogonalization by Gram-Schmidt (factorization into A = QR)
    Properties of determinants (leading to the cofactor formula and the sum over all n! permutations, applications to inv(A) and volume)
    Eigenvalues and eigenvectors (diagonalizing A, computing powers A^k and matrix exponentials to solve difference and differential equations)
    Symmetric matrices and positive definite matrices (real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors, tests for x’Ax > 0, applications)
    Linear transformations and change of basis (connected to the Singular Value Decomposition - orthonormal bases that diagonalize A)
    Linear algebra in engineering (graphs and networks, Markov matrices, Fourier matrix, Fast Fourier Transform, linear programming)

This unit covers the technical skills required to construct argument diagrams using software, including mapping complex structures, linked premises, and implied claims. Students will learn to evaluate arguments for validity and strength using deductive and non-deductive reasoning. Finally, the material applies these skills to analyze public arguments by identifying rhetorical elements and diagramming longer texts.

This course provides a comprehensive foundation in formal logic, beginning with the core concepts of statements and arguments before progressing through the syntax, semantics, and proof systems of sentential logic. Students develop the ability to symbolize natural language into logical formulae, construct truth tables, and build formal derivations using inference rules. The course then extends these skills into predicate logic, where students learn to handle quantifiers, variables, and more complex logical structures. Advanced topics including identity, functions, and metamathematical concepts round out the course, equipping students with rigorous tools for logical analysis and proof construction.
Skill area: Apply logical and critical thinking to evaluate evidence, assumptions, and implications of academic arguments and engineering decisions.

Stated learning outcomes:
Identification, definition, and navigation of the data and information landscape
In order to make sense of the sheer volume of data and information at our disposal, we need to be able to identify, define, and navigate the contemporary information and data landscape. With the rising prominence of Generative Artificial Intelligence (Generative AI) within this landscape, this necessarily includes information-generating tools.

Critical evaluation of data and information
In today’s digital world, we are inundated with data and with the information, disinformation, and misinformation that result when we (mis)interpret them. We need to be able to critically evaluate data sources and collection, how data are presented, and possible limitations on their use.  Using trusted and emerging methods for critical evaluation facilitate scholarly and professional contributions and allow us to be informed and productive members of society.

Data analysis and information engagement in and across communities of practice
Regardless of their major, all students should have a basic understanding of data and data analysis across disciplinary perspectives. This includes an awareness of foundational frameworks that are used in quantitative data analyses at a conceptual level. Additionally, students should be aware of the affordances and basic approaches to qualitative data analysis, which use thematic and interpretive methods to reveal trends and relationships in text-based or image-based data.

Managing the data and information ecosystem consistent with laws, policies, and ethical practices
The processes of obtaining and analyzing data/information and subsequently reporting and documenting the results of those analyses carry with them an obligation for ethical handling and management. Familiarity with the context in which the data and information was previously collected, has previously or is currently being used, and how it should be stored once analysis is complete is a key component of this competency domain.
Stated prerequisites: The ability to read, analyze, and communicate with data, and to make decisions and discern meaning
Intended audience: Current and prospective students at Carnegie Mellon University (CMU).
Skill area: Locate relevant academic and professional information sources and evaluate their quality, reliability, and relevance.
Comments: This course provides essential skills for navigating today’s information-rich and data-driven world. It helps students learn how to identify, evaluate, analyze, and responsibly use information and data across disciplines. By emphasizing critical thinking, ethical practices, and practical data engagement, the course prepares students to become informed professionals, responsible citizens, and lifelong learners.

Resource title: Reading to Write: Research Articles Across the Disciplines
Stated learning outcomes:
By the time they finish this course, learners will learn or be able to:

Read a research article in their discipline effectively.
Deconstruct a research article in their preferred journal.
Recognize and label the individual segments that are used to construct a research article.
Understand the grammatical, lexical, and citation choices made by research article authors.
Make discipline and journal appropriate choices regarding the writing of their own research articles.
Intended audience: Graduate students and highly motivated undergraduate students
Skill area: Locate relevant academic and professional information sources and evaluate their quality, reliability, and relevance.
Comments: This course provides valuable training in how to engage deeply with scholarly research within one’s discipline. By teaching students how to locate target journals and systematically analyze research articles section by section, it strengthens both critical reading and analytical skills. The structured breakdown of introductions, methods, results, and discussions helps students understand not only the content of research but also the conventions, language choices, and expectations of high-quality academic writing. Overall, the course is highly practical and prepares students to become more effective researchers and future contributors to academic publications.

Intended audience: Graduate students and highly motivated undergraduate students
Skill area: Locate relevant academic and professional information sources and evaluate their quality, reliability, and relevance.

By guiding learners through the full review process from understanding review types to developing rigorous search strategies and writing a publishable methods section; it builds strong research and information literacy skills. Its modular, self-paced format makes it accessible while maintaining a clear focus on methodological rigor and scholarly standards.

Resource title: Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis: A Campbell Collaboration Online Course
Stated learning outcomes: The course provides a general overview of all aspects of a scientific literature review, including formulating a problem, finding the relevant literature, coding studies, and meta-analysis. It follows guidelines and standards developed by the Campbell Collaboration, based on empirical evidence about how to produce the most comprehensive and accurate reviews of research. It will help students prepare systematic reviews that include meta-analysis.

Skill area: Locate relevant academic and professional information sources and evaluate their quality, reliability, and relevance.

It emphasizes methodological rigor, bias reduction, and proper synthesis of research findings, making it highly valuable for advanced academic and research work. The focus on both conceptual understanding and statistical tools ensures that participants develop practical skills applicable to high-quality scholarly research.

Resource title: How to Use Storytelling in Academic Writing
Stated learning outcomes:
Implement Narrative Structure: Apply Freytag’s Pyramid (Exposition, Rising Action, Climax, Falling Action, and Dénouement) to both quantitative and qualitative article structures.
Construct Theoretical Characters: Define and organize theoretical constructs as Main Characters (IVs and DVs), Supporting Characters (moderators/mediators), and Ensemble Members (control variables).
Draft Effective Methods: Create a "recipe" for the study that justifies choices and addresses the "Three-Horned Dilemma" of precision, generalizability, and realism.
Ensure Scientific Validity: Provide the necessary information to satisfy four types of validity: Internal, External, Construct, and Statistical Conclusion.
Communicate Results Clearly: Restate and test hypotheses in a parallel structure while interpreting effect sizes and practical influence rather than just p-values.
Execute the "Three Cs": Achieve Completeness, Clarity, and Credibility in the writing of both the Methods and Results sections.
Manage Robustness and Post-Hoc Analysis: Identify potential threats to findings and explain surprise results or non-findings through structured analysis.
Intended audience: Academics and researchers looking to improve their writing for publication (both quantitative and qualitative researchers)
Skill area: Design and deliver accurate, concise, well-structured technical presentations and documents that synthesize complex information and present it in a clear, logical format.
Comments: This course provides a creative and structured approach to academic writing by teaching students how to frame their research as a compelling narrative. Using techniques like Freytag’s Pyramid and relatable analogies, it helps clarify complex sections such as Methods and Results. The course emphasizes completeness, clarity, and credibility, equipping students to communicate their research effectively and increase its impact in their field.

Stated learning outcomes:

Apply the four principles to improve readability, persuasiveness, and clarity in academic papers.
Intended audience: Graduate students or early-career researchers
Skill area: Design and deliver accurate, concise, well-structured technical presentations and documents that synthesize complex information and present it in a clear, logical format.
Comments: This course teaches students how to transform quantitative Methods & Results sections into a compelling, coherent story. Evaluate limits of various functions, including polynomial, rational, trigonometric, logarithmic, and exponential functions, using multiple techniques such as direct substitution, factoring, and the squeeze theorem.

    Apply integral calculus to solve real-world problems, including calculating areas between curves, volumes of solids, arc lengths, and solving problems related to work, energy, and probability.
